name: Total Cholesterol (TC)
summary: Total cholesterol is the sum of cholesterol carried in all lipoprotein particles in the blood — roughly LDL + HDL + VLDL. It is a broad screening number from a standard lipid panel.
whatItMeans: A higher total cholesterol value is generally associated with greater long-term cardiovascular risk, but the number can be misleading on its own because it lumps together atherogenic particles (LDL, VLDL) and protective HDL. A very high total may reflect high LDL, while a high HDL can also nudge the total upward. Because of this, clinicians look at the component fractions (LDL, HDL, triglycerides) rather than the total alone.
raises: Diet high in saturated and trans fats
lowers: Statins and other lipid-lowering medications (ezetimibe, PCSK9 inhibitors)
Diet rich in fiber, unsaturated fats, and plant sterols
confounders: Recent acute illness or infection can transiently lower cholesterol
Severe stress or recent surgery can alter values
Posture (prolonged standing) and prolonged tourniquet time can modestly elevate measured values
Seasonal variation (often slightly higher in winter)

ranges.note: Desirable < 200 mg/dL; 200–239 borderline high; ≥ 240 high (NCEP ATP III).

references.title: Cholesterol testing and results: MedlinePlus Medical Encyclopedia
Mayo Clinic Q and A: Cholesterol — know your numbers
